items.correct: Energy used to maintain basic bodily functions at complete rest.
items.why: Students think BMR is about exercise. It's the baseline energy needed just to STAY ALIVE - breathing, heart, brain, organ function - typically 60-70% of total daily energy use even before any activity.

items.correct: To develop gluten - the protein network that gives dough elasticity and traps gas.
items.why: Students think kneading is just mixing. Mixing happens earlier. Kneading specifically develops gluten strands, aligning them to create the stretchy network that holds CO2 bubbles during rising.

items.q: What is the difference between a food allergy and an intolerance?
items.correct: An allergy involves the immune system; intolerance does not.
items.misconception: They are the same with different severity.
items.random_wrong: Allergies are temporary, intolerances are permanent.
items.why: Students treat these as severity points on one spectrum. They have different mechanisms - allergies trigger immune (IgE) responses (potentially fatal anaphylaxis); intolerances are digestive (e.g., lactase deficiency).

items.q: How long is exclusive breastfeeding recommended?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 6 months. “3 months” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which nutrient is especially recommended pre-conception?
items.why: Correct answer: folate. “vitamin K” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The real figure is 400 µg. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
